title: Chapter 4: Clifftop Observatory
content: This chapter assumes the characters visit the observatory after exploring Seagrow Caves and the wreck of Compass Rose, and thus have reached 3rd level. If they come here before visiting the other adventure sites, the combat encounters are probably too difficult for them. That's the reason Runara won't give the characters the moonstone key or information about the observatory until after they've dealt with the island's other problems. If necessary, you can have Runara warn the characters that they're not ready to face this part of the adventure yet.

children.title: Observatory Overview
children.content:
During its heyday, the Clifftop Observatory was a marvel of magical innovation. Adorned with gorgeous stained glass windows and marble spires, the observatory's towers stood high above the churning sea on separate islets, linked by shimmering bridges of magical energy. But the observatory was wrecked when Runara battled her blue dragon rival centuries ago, and now it lies in ruin, a crumbled memory overtaken by nature.
In recent months, the observatory ruins have become the home of an arrogant blue dragon wyrmling called Sparkrender, a descendant of Runara's ancient rival. Like his ancestor, Sparkrender seeks to claim the destructive magic that lingers at the site. Like Runara, Sparkrender has recruited kobolds to join his cause.
About five days before the characters arrived on the island, a bronze dragon wyrmling named Aidron left the safety of Dragon's Rest and Runara's tutelage and came to the ancient observatory. The two wyrmlings met and immediately clashed. Sparkrender attempted to turn the bronze wyrmling against Runara, but Aidron's hatred of chromatic dragons was stronger than his disagreement with Runara. Ultimately, Sparkrender overpowered Aidron and imprisoned him in the observatory ruins.
Sparkrender believes Aidron is the key to unlocking the observatory's magic and claiming his ancestor's power. With the bronze wyrmling imprisoned, Sparkrender began planning a ritual that will awaken the spirits of all the island's dead dragons and bind them to his will—at the cost of Aidron's life.

children.title: Observatory Features
children.content:
The observatory is built on a series of basalt spires jutting up from the ocean at the southeastern tip of Stormwreck Isle. Almost all the structure's former ceilings and walls have crumbled away, exposing the ruins to the open air.
Unless otherwise noted, the ruins stand 30 feet above the ocean's surface. The rugged cliffs offer abundant hand- and footholds, so a character who jumps or falls into the water and survives (see "Falling" in the Basic Rules) can climb back up without needing to make an ability check.
children.spatial_summary:
[Observatory Features]: **[Overview]**: The Observatory is a ruined structure built atop basalt spires at the southeastern tip of Stormwreck Isle. The main ruins are exposed to the open air and stand 30 feet above the ocean.

**[Direct Connections]**: The ocean surface is 30 feet below the main ruins. The rugged cliffs provide easy climbing access from the water back to the ruins.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Stormwreck Isle**: The larger island containing the observatory.
  - **The Observatory Ruins**: The primary ruined structure on the basalt spires. It is exposed to the open air.
    - **The Ocean Surface**: The water 30 feet below the main ruins, accessible by falling or jumping. The cliffs allow for easy climbing back up.

[Observatory Overview]: **[Overview]**: The Clifftop Observatory is a ruined magical structure located on basalt spires at the southeastern tip of Stormwreck Isle. Its main towers stand on separate islets, connected by ruined magical bridges, 30 feet above the ocean.

**[Direct Connections]**: The ocean surface is 30 feet below the main ruins. The rugged cliffs provide easy climbing access from the water back to the ruins.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Stormwreck Isle**: The larger island.
  - **Clifftop Observatory Ruins**: The primary ruined structure on the basalt spires, now home to Sparkrender and his kobolds. It contains the following:
    - **Observatory Towers**: The main towers on separate islets, now crumbling and overgrown.
    - **Ruined Magical Bridges**: The remnants of the shimmering bridges that once connected the towers.
    - **Aidron's Prison**: The specific area within the ruins where the bronze wyrmling is imprisoned.
  - **The Ocean Surface**: The water 30 feet below the main ruins, accessible by falling or jumping. The cliffs allow for easy climbing back up.

children.title: Running This Chapter
children.content:
Once the characters decide to visit the Clifftop Observatory, they have three options for reaching the site:
- **By Boat**: Dragon's Rest has a rowboat the characters can take around the eastern end of the island. The trip to the observatory is 5 miles, which takes about 3 hours and 20 minutes to row.
- **Along the Coast**: Walking around the coast of the island is a little easier than rowing, and a quicker trip despite the characters having to walk around the eastern bay instead of rowing across it. The 6-mile trip takes only 2 hours at a normal walking pace.
- **Overland**: The characters can walk southeast from Dragon's Rest to the head of the bay, where a rough path cuts across the island to the eastern bay. The rocky ground of the island is difficult terrain, but this is still the fastest and most direct route. Characters can make the 3½-mile trip in about 1 hour and 15 minutes.

children.title: Approaching by Land
children.content:
If the characters approach the observatory by land, read the following text:
The blue shape is Sparkrender flying by. This glimpse of the dragon is an opportunity to make your players nervous about the foe they're about to face. Feel free to elaborate on the description of Sparkrender and play on the characters' worry that he might spot them, but ultimately he passes by without noticing them. The characters see him come to rest on a rocky spire off the coast as he returns to his hoard among the ruins (area D5).
A character who examines the crystalline protrusions or the scarred vegetation can make a 10 Intelligence (Nature) check. On a success, the character recognizes the phenomenon as a sign of lightning strikes—or the lightning breath of a blue or bronze dragon.
Assuming the characters continue toward the observatory, they soon arrive at area D1, described below.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: Clambering over the rocky ground of Stormwreck Isle, you spot strange, twisted protrusions of glassy crystal jutting from the earth. The vegetation in this area bears reddish branching scars that form similar shapes. Suddenly, you hear a screeching roar and notice a winged, glittering blue shape swooping overhead.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The current text describes a general area of Stormwreck Isle's rocky ground, characterized by crystalline formations and scarred vegetation. The node "Untitled Section" is not a distinct, named location but a narrative scene. Therefore, no node is created for it, and any valid spatial children from its sub-reports are promoted to the top level. The provided sub-area reports are empty.

**[Direct Connections]**: None specified in the text.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Crystalline Scarlands of Stormwreck Isle** (Descriptive Name): A rocky, open area on the island's surface, marked by protrusions of glassy crystal and vegetation bearing reddish, crystalline scarring patterns. A glittering blue dragon is seen flying overhead.

[Approaching by Land]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "Approaching by Land," is a narrative scene describing a journey across a rocky area of Stormwreck Isle. It is not a distinct, named location. Therefore, no node is created for it, and its valid spatial children are promoted.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text states that characters continuing toward the observatory soon arrive at **area D1**.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Crystalline Scarlands of Stormwreck Isle** (Descriptive Name): A rocky, open area on the island's surface, marked by protrusions of glassy crystal and vegetation bearing reddish, crystalline scarring patterns. A glittering blue dragon is seen flying overhead. It connects to **area D1**.
- **D1. The Observatory Entrance**: The destination for characters who continue from the Scarlands.

children.title: Approaching by Water
children.content:
If the characters row to the observatory, read this text instead:
Again, Sparkrender does not notice the characters as he returns to his lair, but feel free to tease the players with the idea that he might notice them.
If the characters beach the boat on the shore, they can easily climb the bluffs to area D1, described below. If they tie up the boat at the base of one of the pillars, they can instead climb to the area atop it (area D2, D3, D4, or D5).

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content:
As you round the southeastern tip of the island, you can see crumbling ruins atop basalt columns just off the main island body. If you pull the boat ashore on the island, it would be an easy walk up to the top of the cliffs, though there is no obvious bridge from the cliffs to the ruins. Alternatively, you could tie the boat up at the bottom of the columns and try to climb directly up to the ruins.
As you consider these options, you hear a screeching roar and notice a winged, glittering blue shape swooping through the air ahead of you. The figure comes to rest atop the column farthest from Stormwreck Isle and vanishes from sight.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The text describes a coastal area of Stormwreck Isle, focusing on a ruin site separated from the main island by water. No sub-area reports were provided.

**[Direct Connections]**: The location is reached by boat from the waters around Stormwreck Isle. Two potential points of arrival are mentioned: 1) the main island shore (for an approach from the cliffs), and 2) the base of the basalt columns (for a direct climb).

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Stormwreck Isle (Southeastern Coast)**: The mainland area from which the ruins are viewed. A boat can be pulled ashore here for an overland approach.
- **The Basalt Column Ruins**: A crumbling ruin complex built atop a cluster of basalt columns just off the island's southeastern tip. It is separated from the main island by a gap. It can be approached by climbing the columns from the water below or potentially accessed from the adjacent cliffs. The column farthest from Stormwreck Isle holds a specific perch.

[Approaching by Water]: **[Overview]**: The text describes the approach to the Basalt Column Ruins by boat, offering two arrival points: the main island shore or the base of the pillars.

**[Direct Connections]**: From the water, a boat can be beached on the **Southeastern Shore of Stormwreck Isle** or tied up at the **Base of the Basalt Columns**.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Stormwreck Isle (Southeastern Coast)**: The mainland shore. A boat can be beached here, allowing a climb up the bluffs to **The Clifftop (D1)**.
- **The Basalt Column Ruins**: A ruin complex built atop basalt columns offshore. A boat can be tied at the base of a pillar to climb directly to the top of one.
  - **The Clifftop (D1)**: Accessible by climbing the bluffs from the beached boat on the main island shore.
  - **Pillar Top (D2, D3, D4, or D5)**: Accessible by climbing a specific column from a boat tied at its base.

children.title: Sparkrender's Kobold Allies
children.content:
The kobolds who joined forces with Sparkrender are lawful evil and cruel. Their initial reaction toward outsiders is hostile (see "Social Interaction" in the Basic Rules), but they're more likely to warn intruders to leave the ruins than to immediately attack. They readily threaten violence and back up their threats with combat if necessary.
As an action, a character can try to convince a hostile kobold to have a conversation or to allow the characters to explore the area, doing so on a successful 15 Charisma check. Depending on the character's approach, the Deception, Intimidation, or Persuasion skill can apply to the check.
This list summarizes what the kobolds know:
- **Kobold History**: The kobolds here have served Sparkrender for about a year. They revere the blue dragon as a semidivine figure and trust him to provide for their every need.
- **Big Plans**: Sparkrender has big plans that will allow him to manifest his full power. The wyrmling is waiting for the sculpture in the rotunda ruins (area D2) to tell him it's time to act. The kobolds think it must be soon—possibly even today.
- **Dragon Visitor**: Not long ago, another dragon arrived. This other dragon was about Sparkrender's size but looked like greenish-yellow metal. They remember hearing the two arguing at the observatory tower (area D5), and they have heard the other dragon roaring and crashing around inside the tower (area D6) since then.
- **Caved-In Wall**: Though the kobolds have been trying to tidy the ruins, Sparkrender specifically told them not to clean up the newly caved-in wall at the base of the observatory tower (area D6). The caved-in wall is visible from area D3.
children.spatial_summary:
[Sparkrender's Kobold Allies]: **[Overview]**: The text describes the kobolds' knowledge of several key locations within the ruins they inhabit. The node "Sparkrender's Kobold Allies" is not a physical location itself, but a faction. Therefore, its valid spatial children are promoted to the top level.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text explicitly connects the "observatory tower" (area D5) to the interior space where the other dragon is (area D6). It also notes that the "caved-in wall at the base of the observatory tower (area D6)" is visible from area D3.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
  - **Rotunda Ruins (Area D2)**: Contains a significant sculpture that Sparkrender is waiting on.
  - **Observatory Tower (Area D5)**: A tower where Sparkrender and another dragon were heard arguing.
    - **Tower Interior / Dragon's Chamber (Area D6)**: The interior of the observatory tower, where the other dragon currently resides. Its base has a caved-in wall.
  - **Area D3**: A location from which the caved-in wall at the base of Area D6 is visible.

children.title: Observatory Locations
children.content:
The following locations are keyed to map 5, which shows the layout of the Clifftop Observatory.
**Gallery:**
[Image (map): Map 5: Clifftop Observatory]
[Image (mapPlayer): Player Version]

children.title: D1: Overlook
children.content:
At the base of each statue is a small hexagonal indentation, about 1 inch wide and 2 inches deep—perfectly sized to accommodate the moonstone key Runara gave them.
When the key is inserted into the base of either statue, magical light sparks to life in both statues' open mouths, and a shimmering bridge made of iridescent magical energy extends from the overlook to the observatory ruins (area D2). The bridge is sturdy and lasts until the key is removed from the statue.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: A broken and overgrown path winds to the edge of the cliff. The overlook is marked by two marble statues veined with gold, each carved in the shape of a dragon with its mouth open in a silent roar.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "Untitled Section," describes a specific physical location: a cliffside overlook at the end of a broken path, marked by two distinctive statues. As it is a valid location, it will be the parent node. No sub-area reports were provided.

**[Direct Connections]**: The location is connected to a "broken and overgrown path" that winds to it. The path is the implied approach.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Gilded Roar Overlook (Untitled Section)**: A cliffside overlook marked by two gold-veined marble dragon statues, mouths open in a silent roar. It is reached by a broken, overgrown path.
    - **The Broken Approach (Path)**: The overgrown and broken path leading to the overlook.

[D1: Overlook]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "D1: Overlook," is a specific physical location. The provided sub-area report for the "Untitled Section" describes the same location, which will be integrated and given a synthesized name.

**[Direct Connections]**: A shimmering bridge of iridescent magical energy extends from this overlook to the observatory ruins (area D2). The overlook is reached by a broken and overgrown path.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Gilded Roar Overlook (D1)**: A cliffside overlook marked by two gold-veined marble dragon statues, mouths open in a silent roar. It is reached by a broken, overgrown path and is the origin point for a magical bridge to D2.
    - **The Broken Approach (Path)**: The overgrown and broken path leading to the overlook.
- **Observatory Ruins (D2)**: Connected to the overlook via a magical bridge.

children.title: D2: Rotunda Ruins
children.content:
The two Winged Kobold are locked in battle with eight Stirge. When the characters enter the area, six of the stirges turn their attention to this new, juicier prey. The kobolds try to deal with the remaining two stirges, and then decide what to do based on what the characters are doing. If the characters actively help the kobolds during the battle, then the kobolds return the favor. If the characters attack the kobolds instead of (or as well as) the stirges, the kobolds fight back. Otherwise, the kobolds hang back, trying to stay out of the way until the fight is over.
Once the stirges have been defeated, if the kobolds survive, they approach the characters. Their names are Mek and Minn, and they're the brothers of Myla, the kobold tinkerer at Dragon's Rest. They left their sister for dead after the stirge attack that maimed her wings. They have sworn loyalty to Sparkrender, and they share the dragon's cruel, haughty demeanor.
If the characters actively helped the kobolds or mention that Myla is alive, the kobolds' attitude becomes friendly. They offer to introduce the characters to their leader and help the characters in any way they can (such as retrieving the moonstone key from area D1 so the characters can activate the bridge to D4). They won't help the characters fight Sparkrender, though; they are loyal to the dragon.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content:
Broken stone lines this plaza—fragments of elegant statues, once-magnificent pillars, and shining marble walls. At the center, a tall sculpture of rusted planets and gilded stars spins idly in a jerky mimicry of celestial motion.
A gargled screech suddenly rises from across the plaza. A half-dozen batlike creatures are swarming around two winged kobolds with blue paint smeared across their long snouts. The kobolds are fighting fiercely, but they seem close to being overwhelmed.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The text describes a single, distinct location: a ruined plaza with a central sculpture. No explicit connections to other areas are mentioned. There are no valid child nodes to promote.

**[Direct Connections]**: None explicitly stated.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Shattered Plaza (A1)**: A ruined ceremonial space paved with broken stone, featuring shattered statues, pillars, and marble walls. At its center is a tall, rusted sculpture of planets and stars that spins erratically. The plaza is currently the site of a skirmish between winged kobolds and batlike creatures.

children.title: Golden Sculpture
children.content:
The sculpture in the center of the rotunda is an astronomical model used for research in centuries past. The sculpture depicts the planet of Toril (the world of the Forgotten Realms), its moon (called Selûne), the sun, and seven other planets, as well as one comet with a very eccentric orbit. A character who studies the sculpture can puzzle out its importance with a successful 15 Intelligence (Arcana) check: its current position suggests the comet will soon pass very close to Toril.
Sparkrender plans to hold his ritual when the comet is at its closest point. He believes the comet, called the King-Killer Star, controls the destiny of dragons and will allow him to claim the power of the dragons who fell on Stormwreck Isle.
children.spatial_summary:
[Golden Sculpture]: **[Overview]**: The Golden Sculpture is a detailed astronomical model located in the center of a rotunda. It is a physical landmark within a larger structure.

**[Direct Connections]**: The sculpture is located in the center of a rotunda. The rotunda itself is the immediate containing space.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Rotunda**: A chamber containing the Golden Sculpture at its center.
  - **Golden Sculpture (Landmark)**: A large, intricate model of the Torilian solar system, mounted in the center of the rotunda.

children.title: Dragon Effigies
children.content:
The stirge attack interrupted the kobolds from their assigned task of preparing this site for Sparkrender's ritual. With the wyrmling's help, they have crudely sculpted five chunks of rubble into vaguely draconic shapes and splashed each one with paint, and the kobolds were in the process of arranging them around the metal sculpture. Each effigy has the name of the dragon it represents etched into it. These are the names and colors of the five dragons:
Astalagan | Bronze
Clyssavar | Gold
Eldenemir | Blue
Sharruth | Red
Turadaer | Brass
If the characters ask the kobolds about these dragon effigies, Mek and Minn swell with pride and explain that they crafted them according to Sparkrender's instructions. The kobolds know the effigies have some part to play in Sparkrender's plans.
children.spatial_summary:
[Dragon Effigies]: **[Overview]**: The Dragon Effigies are a set of five crudely sculpted and painted stone objects, arranged around the central metal sculpture in the ritual site.

**[Direct Connections]**: The effigies are arranged around the central metal sculpture.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Ritual Site (Promoted)**: The area where the kobolds were preparing for Sparkrender's ritual. Contains the central metal sculpture and the five Dragon Effigies arranged around it.
  - **Dragon Effigy (Astalagan | Bronze)**: A painted stone effigy.
  - **Dragon Effigy (Clyssavar | Gold)**: A painted stone effigy.
  - **Dragon Effigy (Eldenemir | Blue)**: A painted stone effigy.
  - **Dragon Effigy (Sharruth | Red)**: A painted stone effigy.
  - **Dragon Effigy (Turadaer | Brass)**: A painted stone effigy.

children.title: Energy Bridge Anchors
children.content: A pair of dragon statues like the ones in area D1 stands on the west side of the rotunda, and another pair stands on the southwest side. If the moonstone key is inserted in one of these statues, the western pair creates a bridge back to the overlook (area D1), and the southwestern pair creates a bridge to a crumbled and isolated tower (area D4).
children.spatial_summary:
[Energy Bridge Anchors]: **[Overview]**: The node describes a set of statues within a rotunda that function as anchors for magical bridges. The statues themselves are not a distinct location but a feature of a larger space.

**[Direct Connections]**: The western pair of statues creates a bridge to **The Overlook (Area D1)**. The southwestern pair creates a bridge to **The Crumbled Tower (Area D4)**.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
  - **The Rotunda**: Contains the four dragon statues that serve as the Energy Bridge Anchors.
    - **The Overlook (Area D1)**: Connected via a magical bridge from the western statues.
    - **The Crumbled Tower (Area D4)**: Connected via a magical bridge from the southwestern statues.

[D2: Rotunda Ruins]: **[Overview]**: The current node describes a location called the "Rotunda Ruins" (D2). The provided sub-area reports describe its internal features and connections, but treat them as separate, sometimes overlapping, hierarchies. This synthesis creates a single, unified spatial report for D2.

**[Direct Connections]**: The western pair of dragon statues creates a magical bridge to **The Overlook (Area D1)**. The southwestern pair creates a magical bridge to **The Crumbled Tower (Area D4)**.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **D2: Rotunda Ruins**: A large, ruined circular chamber or plaza.
  - **The Shattered Plaza**: The main floor of the rotunda, paved with broken stone and littered with shattered statues and pillars.
    - **The Golden Sculpture (Landmark)**: A large, intricate, and rusted model of the Torilian solar system, mounted at the center of the plaza. It spins erratically.
    - **Dragon Effigy Circle**: A ritual site within the plaza where five crudely painted stone dragon effigies (Astalagan/Bronze, Clyssavar/Gold, Eldenemir/Blue, Sharruth/Red, Turadaer/Brass) are arranged around the central Golden Sculpture.
  - **Energy Bridge Anchors**: Four dragon statues positioned around the rotunda's perimeter. The western and southwestern pairs are active, creating bridges to other areas.
    - **The Overlook (Area D1)**: Connected via a magical bridge from the western statues.
    - **The Crumbled Tower (Area D4)**: Connected via a magical bridge from the southwestern statues.

children.title: D3: Kobold Camp
children.content:
A rickety bridge made of driftwood and rope spans the 15-foot gap between the rotunda (area D2) and this structure.
Three Kobold (Ekrash, Erp, and Hev) and two Winged Kobold (Nuhro and Snirke) currently inhabit this camp, polishing Sling Bullet and keeping busy until it's time for Sparkrender's ritual. Initially, the kobolds are hostile toward the characters, determined to scare off the intruders. They are susceptible to intimidation, though; as an action, a character can make a 13 Charisma (Intimidation) check, convincing them to back down on a success.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: Skittering sounds and whispers come from inside this ruined tower. Gaps in the stone are patched over with wooden planks and threadbare cloth.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "Untitled Section", is a descriptive passage about a ruined tower. It is a valid physical location. No sub-area reports were provided.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text does not specify any explicit exits or connections to other named locations.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Ruined Tower**: A dilapidated stone structure with gaps patched by wood and cloth. Skittering sounds and whispers emanate from within.

[D3: Kobold Camp]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "D3: Kobold Camp", is a valid physical location. The provided sub-area report for an "Untitled Section" describes a separate, distinct location (a ruined tower) that is not explicitly connected to the Kobold Camp in the given text. It is treated as a sibling location.

**[Direct Connections]**:
- **Rotunda (Area D2)**: Connected via a rickety driftwood and rope bridge spanning a 15-foot gap.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:

children.title: D4: Isolated Study
children.content: No bridge spans the 22-foot gap between the rotunda and this spire, unless the characters use the moonstone key to activate the energy bridge in area D2. Characters might come up with any number of ingenious ways to cross the gap. See the sidebar called "Clever Solutions" for advice to help you determine whether these solutions work.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: The rhythm of the waves below echoes throughout this cramped tower. Part of the floor has crumbled away, dropping off into a chamber below. Amid the rubble, collapsed bookcases jut out at odd angles, and moldering books are strewn across the floor.
children.spatial_summary:
[Untitled Section]: **[Overview]**: The current text describes a single, distinct physical location: a ruined tower room overlooking the sea. There are no sub-area reports to process.

**[Direct Connections]**: The only explicit spatial connection is a vertical drop through the crumbled floor into a **chamber below**.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Crumbling Tower Room**: A cramped, ruined chamber in a seaside tower. The floor has partially collapsed, creating a drop into a lower chamber. The room is littered with rubble, collapsed bookcases, and moldering books. The rhythm of the waves echoes here.
  - **Chamber Below (A1)**: Accessed via a drop through the crumbled floor of the tower room above.

children.title: Scholar's Journal
children.content:
Though most of the books have fallen apart, one tome—a small black journal with an ornate lock—remains intact. A character who examines the book and succeeds on a 15 Wisdom (Perception) check notices a small arcane rune engraved above the keyhole on the journal's lock. A character who casts the detect magic spell also sees a faint aura around the keyhole.
This rune is a magical trap that triggers if the journal is opened without the use of its original key, which is long lost. A character who makes a successful 11 Intelligence (Arcana) check can determine how to disarm the trap: by carefully scratching over the rune with a dagger, a sharp piece of wire, or a similar implement. Once this is done, the journal can safely be opened either by picking the lock with thieves' tools and making a successful 10 Dexterity check, or by breaking the lock with a successful 12 Strength check. If the lock is opened without first disarming the trap, the magic trap spews out noxious green gas, and the character who opened the lock takes 3 (1d6) poison damage.
Inside, the journal contains star maps and notes on experiments with magic. One passage is underlined near the start of the journal. It reads, in an archaic dialect of Common, "To ye four scholars: point your eyes toward the Dragon's light, for it guides your descent into knowledge." This passage is a clue to unlocking the hidden entrance in the observatory tower (area D5).

children.title: Clever Solutions
children.content:
The challenges characters face in this adventure can have more than one solution. For example, to reach the isolated study (area D4), characters could try any of these approaches:
- Ask the winged kobolds to retrieve the moonstone key from area D1 so it can be used again in area D2.
- Dive or climb down into the water below, swim over to the other column, and climb back up to the study.
- Have a character use the misty step spell to cross the gap and explore alone.
Remember, there's more than one way to proceed through an adventure, and more than one way to play D&D! When characters come up with clever solutions to tricky problems, reward them with success—or at least a good chance of success. Use the guidelines in the "Improvising Ability Checks" section to help you. Embrace your players' creativity, and let them surprise you!
children.spatial_summary:
[Clever Solutions]: **[Overview]**: The text describes a spatial puzzle involving an isolated study on a column, separated by a gap over water. The solutions involve movement between specific, named areas.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text explicitly connects three areas: the "isolated study (area D4)", "area D1", and "area D2". The study is separated from the main area by a gap over water.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Isolated Study (Area D4)**: A study located on a stone column. It is separated from the main area by a significant gap over water below. It can be accessed via flight, swimming/climbing, or teleportation.
- **Area D1**: A location containing a "moonstone key," which can be retrieved by winged kobolds and used in Area D2.
- **Area D2**: A location where the retrieved moonstone key from Area D1 can be used.

[Scholar's Journal]: **[Overview]**: The current node describes a single object (a journal) and its contents, not a physical location. The sub-area report from "Clever Solutions" provides the only spatial information, describing an isolated study and its connections to other areas.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text for "Scholar's Journal" does not describe any physical connections between locations.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Isolated Study (Area D4)**: A study located on a stone column, separated from the main area by a gap over water. It can be accessed via flight, swimming/climbing, or teleportation.
- **Area D1**: A location containing a "moonstone key," which can be retrieved by winged kobolds and used in Area D2.
- **Area D2**: A location where the retrieved moonstone key from Area D1 can be used.

children.title: Treasure
children.content: A character who searches the tower and succeeds on a 12 Intelligence (Investigation) check finds a loose brick in the northwest wall. Pulling the brick out reveals a hidden compartment containing a Potion of Lightning Resistance (described in appendix A) and a pouch holding 10 gp.
children.spatial_summary:
[Treasure]: **[Overview]**: The "Treasure" node is not a physical location; it is a container of objects. Its spatial context is a "tower," specifically a loose brick in the northwest wall of that tower. The treasure's location is promoted to the parent node.

**[Direct Connections]**: The treasure is located within a hidden compartment in the northwest wall of a tower.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Tower**: Contains a hidden compartment in its northwest wall.
  - **Northwest Wall (Hidden Compartment)**: Contains a Potion of Lightning Resistance and a pouch of 10 gp.

[D4: Isolated Study]: **[Overview]**: The current node, "D4: Isolated Study," is a valid physical location. The sub-area reports provide additional spatial context for this location and its connections, as well as a separate, lower chamber. The "Treasure" node's spatial context is promoted to this location.

**[Direct Connections]**: The study is isolated on a stone column. The only explicit connection is a 22-foot gap over water to a "rotunda" (presumably Area D2). This gap can be crossed via an energy bridge activated by a moonstone key from Area D1, or by other means (flight, swimming/climbing, teleportation).

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Isolated Study (D4)**: A study on a stone column, separated by a 22-foot gap from a rotunda (Area D2). Contains a hidden compartment in its northwest wall.
  - **Northwest Wall (Hidden Compartment)**: Contains a Potion of Lightning Resistance and a pouch of 10 gp.
- **Chamber Below (A1)**: A lower chamber accessed via a vertical drop through the crumbled floor of a separate, ruined tower room overlooking the sea.

children.title: D5: Observatory Tower
children.content:
Standing taller than the rest of the observatory, the main floor of this tower is 45 feet above the ocean's surface, which puts it 15 feet above the floor of the rotunda (area D2). The kobolds have constructed a rough pulley lift so the wingless kobolds can reach this area when Sparkrender summons them.
Sparkrender the blue dragon wyrmling is currently sleeping here, curled up against the northeast wall. This presents the characters with an important choice: they can waken the dragon and fight him now, or they can sneak past the dragon, find a way into the secret library below (area D6) and free Bronze Dragon Wyrmling, and then fight Sparkrender with Aidron's help. Either approach is fine. The fight will be easier for the characters with Aidron on their side, but they can still triumph without Aidron's help.
You don't need to encourage one course of action or the other. Make sure the players realize they have a choice, though. You can use Mek and Minn (the kobolds from D2) to remind the characters there's another dragon around. As you describe the observatory tower, you can also highlight the caved-in wall beneath it (leading to area D6) as a potential point of access to the tower. Or you can just tell the players they have these two options—plus any others they come up with on their own!

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content:
Rays of light dance across the remains of this tower's stained glass dome, making iridescent colors shimmer across the crumbled marble walls. Gilded lines and jeweled inlays form a detailed star map spanning the dusty floor. Four alabaster statues of scholars stand around the perimeter of the room, their expressions worn with time. Each ten-foot-tall statue is pointing or gesturing in a different direction.
Curled in the northeast corner of the tower is a lithe blue dragon. Lightning arcs around his horns and snout as he slumbers amid a scattered collection of coins and glittering jewels.

**[Direct Connections]**: No explicit exits, doors, or passages are mentioned in the text.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Celestial Observatory (Ruined Tower)**: A circular chamber, approximately 40-50 feet in diameter, defined by crumbled marble walls and the remains of a stained glass dome. The floor is a detailed, gilded star map. Four ten-foot-tall alabaster statues of scholars stand around the perimeter. A lithe blue dragon slumbers in the northeast corner amid a scattered hoard.

children.title: Sneaking Around
children.content:
If the characters move quietly around the area, they can avoid waking Blue Dragon Wyrmling as long as at least half of them succeed on a 14 Dexterity (Stealth) check.
If the characters try to turn the statues (see "Hidden Entrance" below) without waking Sparkrender, have the characters turning the north and east statues (nearest the sleeping dragon) each make a 14 Dexterity (Stealth) check. On a failed check, the noise of a turning statue wakes the dragon.
children.spatial_summary:
[Sneaking Around]: **[Overview]**: The text describes actions taken within a larger area containing a sleeping dragon and statues. "Sneaking Around" is not a physical location but a narrative action. Its associated spatial context is promoted.

**[Direct Connections]**: The text implies movement within a single chamber containing a sleeping dragon (Blue Dragon Wyrmling/Sparkrender) and multiple statues.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Dragon's Lair (Promoted)**: A chamber containing a sleeping Blue Dragon Wyrmling named Sparkrender and several statues. The north and east statues are specifically noted as being nearest the dragon. The area requires stealth to navigate quietly.
  - **Statue Cluster**: A group of statues within the lair, including a north statue and an east statue positioned close to the dragon's resting place. These statues are part of a mechanism (see "Hidden Entrance").

children.title: Waking the Dragon
children.content:
If awakened, Sparkrender is hostile toward the characters, growling and barking threats in Draconic. Characters who speak Draconic (the paladin and the wizard) can try to convince the dragon not to attack by making a 12 Charisma check and applying the Deception, Intimidation, or Persuasion skill as appropriate. A character who succeeds on the check stops Sparkrender from attacking immediately, and he instead takes the opportunity to boast about his grand ambitions. If no character successfully intervenes, or if a character mentions Aidron or Runara, Sparkrender snarls and attacks.
Despite his youth, Blue Dragon Wyrmling is a fearsome foe and a loathsome villain. He uses Breath Weapon on his first turn and every round it is available thereafter. (Clever characters can try to use the statues for cover from the dragon's breath; see "Cover" in the Basic Rules.) Consider having the wyrmling deliver short lines of dialogue on each of his turns, inspired by the following examples:
- "You pests will not stand in my way! I will claim the might of my ancestors!"
- "Get out of here before I tire of this game and end you all!"
- "Chromatic dragons are the rightful rulers of this world. Bow down to the children of Tiamat!"
- "Enough of this! This work is too important to be hindered by the likes of you!"
The dragon fights until reduced to 10 or fewer hit points, at which point he swears vengeance against the characters and attempts to flee the island starting on his next turn. (He might say something like this as he takes the Disengage action and flies away: "I swear by Eldenemir the Raging Storm and the five heads of Tiamat, you will pay for this insult!")
If the characters flee, Sparkrender does not pursue them, but he taunts them as they run away. (He might say something like, "Yes! Flee before my might, as all will flee when I claim my inheritance!")
If the characters defeat Sparkrender, they hear growling and yelping coming from below them (area D6).
children.spatial_summary:
[Waking the Dragon]: **[Overview]**: The node "Waking the Dragon" describes an event (a combat encounter with a dragon) that occurs within a specific location, but the node title itself is not a location. Therefore, its valid spatial children (from sub-area reports) are promoted to the top level. The event text provides spatial context for the encounter area.

**[Direct Connections]**: The encounter area is connected to **area D6** below it, from which growling and yelping can be heard if the dragon is defeated.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Dragon's Lair (Sparkrender's Chamber)**: The location where the encounter with the blue dragon wyrmling Sparkrender takes place. The chamber contains statues that can be used for cover. It is situated above **area D6**.
  - **Area D6**: A sub-level area directly beneath the lair, accessible from it. The sounds of creatures come from here.

children.title: Hidden Entrance
children.content:
Each of the four statues can be rotated on its base. To unlock the hidden entrance to the observatory's secret archive, each statue must be rotated so it points at the constellation called the Dragon of Dawn on the floor's star map.
If the characters found the clue in the isolated study (area D4) exhorting "four scholars" to "point your eyes toward the Dragon's light," they might search the star map for a constellation that looks like a dragon. A character who searches the floor and succeeds on a 10 Intelligence (Investigation) check finds a constellation resembling a dragon in the southeast quadrant of the floor.
In lieu of that information, a character who searches the room for clues and succeeds on a 15 Wisdom (Perception) check notices worn grooves in the base of each statue, suggesting the statues can turn on their bases. While turning a statue, a character can make a 10 Wisdom (Perception) check; if the check succeeds, the character notices the statue settles slightly when it is pointing toward the southeast quadrant of the room and requires extra effort to nudge out of that position.
Once each statue is in its correct position, a semicircular section at the center of the floor begins to glow before descending into the library (area D6). It forms a spiral staircase leading down to the floor of the library. When any statue is rotated out of its position, the staircase rises back up, sealing the library shut once more. (A character on the staircase when this happens is lifted up to this area on the rising stairs.)

- **Secret Archive Library (Area D6)**: The destination at the bottom of the spiral staircase. It is spatially below the Observatory Statue Room.

children.title: Treasure
children.content: With Sparkrender no longer defending his fledgling hoard, characters can gather it up. It includes large piles of coins: 4,500 cp, 2,200 sp, and 130 gp. There are also ten gems: five pale blue quartz crystals worth 10 gp each and five blue jasper stones worth 50 gp each. A waterproof leather case holds a blue silk fan painted with powdered blue gems, worth 25 gp. There are also a few ordinary items Sparkrender enjoyed, including a crude flute with a pleasing sound, an hourglass filled with sparkling sand, and a set of seven candlesticks.

children.title: D6: Secret Library
children.content:
This space was formerly a hidden archive of knowledge, accessible only to those who knew the observatory's secrets. However, the walls of the tower are crumbling—a fact that Sparkrender used to trap Aidron here. After weakening the bronze wyrmling, Sparkrender forced him into the library and then caused one of the walls to cave in, sealing the exit.
If the characters don't use the hidden entrance in area D5, they can attempt to clear away the rubble from the cave-in to access the secret library. To access the rubble, the characters must climb down from area D5 or swim to the bottom of this spire and climb up from the water. Clearing the rubble takes one character 30 minutes, or the characters can work together to clear it faster (for example, it takes two characters 15 minutes or five characters 6 minutes). If the characters are trying to clear the rubble quietly, it takes twice as long, and at least half of the characters must succeed on a 14 Dexterity (Stealth) check to avoid waking Sparkrender.
Once they've cleared the rubble, the characters can access the library's interior.
Aidron the bronze dragon wyrmling excitedly greets the characters when they enter. He has spent days trying to dig his way out through the caved-in wall, but his efforts from the inside only caused further collapse. He is eager to escape, but if the characters ask, he explains his conflict with Sparkrender. He expresses regret over his inability to defeat the blue wyrmling and concern for the safety of the island's other inhabitants. And he is terrified of the fate Sparkrender has in store for him—the blue dragon plans to use Aidron's death to claim the power of all the dead dragons on the island, transforming himself into a mighty draconic avatar.
If the characters have not yet defeated Sparkrender, Aidron decides to face the blue dragon himself. He flies to the top of the observatory tower (area D5) to confront Sparkrender once more. However, Aidron is too weak to defeat Sparkrender alone; he needs the characters' help.
Once Sparkrender is defeated, the bronze wyrmling is excited to return to Dragon's Rest with the characters.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: Stale air heavy with the smell of old parchment floods your nostrils. The walls are lined with shelves stuffed full of old tomes and yellowing scrolls. Glass cases, toppled over and shattered, have strewn their contents across the stone floor. The sound of splintering wood echoes through the space, and a moment later you see an agitated bronze dragon the size of a bear, picking himself up from the wreckage of the old desk he apparently crashed into.

children.title: Sparkrender's Ritual
children.content:
If the characters leave the observatory without defeating Blue Dragon Wyrmling, they might return to find the blue wyrmling's ritual underway. This is most likely to happen if the characters leave and take a long rest before confronting Sparkrender, or if they flee from combat with Sparkrender and return after they've rested.
Another possibility is that the characters drive Sparkrender away from the island without killing him, but leave Aidron imprisoned in area D6. In this case, Runara urges the characters to return to the observatory to find Aidron. When they arrive, Sparkrender has also rested, healed, and returned to finish his work.
In either case, the characters arrive just as Sparkrender's ritual is getting underway. (If they took or destroyed the dragon effigies in area D2, they have been replaced by even cruder versions created in a hurry.) Read the following text:
To stop the ritual, the characters must face Sparkrender (blue dragon wyrmling), potentially with the help of Aidron (bronze dragon wyrmling), if they can free Aidron from his chains (see "Freeing Aidron" below). Any surviving kobolds and winged kobolds lurk here, but they stay out of combat if possible.
See "Waking the Dragon" in area D5 for ideas on how to play Sparkrender in this encounter. He uses Breath Weapon as the characters approach the scene (unleashing lightning into the sky), so he has to wait for this action to recharge before he can use it on the characters this time. He fights to the death—he has too much riding on the success of this ritual to abandon it now.

children.title: Untitled Section
children.content: Streams of colored light swirl through the air around the golden statue in the ruined rotunda. Each shimmering display seems to originate from one of the five dragon effigies you saw before, and the lights' colors match the colors of the effigies: red, gold, brass, blue, and bronze. A blue dragon is perched atop the sculpture, throwing his head back in pain or ecstasy as the lights surround him, and he unleashes a bolt of lightning up toward the sky. At the base of the statue, a bronze dragon is bound to the ground by three heavy chains. He looks like he's in agony.
children.spatial_summary:

**[Direct Connections]**: No explicit exits, doors, or passages are mentioned in the text.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Ruined Rotunda (A)**: A circular, ruined chamber. Its central feature is a golden statue, around which streams of colored light (red, gold, brass, blue, bronze) swirl through the air. A blue dragon is perched atop the statue, and a bronze dragon is chained at its base.

children.title: Untitled Section
Dragon Spirits
children.content:
At the end of each round of combat (on initiative count 0, after everyone else has acted), a random magical effect occurs, caused by the magical lights that swirl around the rotunda. These lights are manifestations of the dragon spirits Sparkrender is trying to bind to himself, but their effects are unpredictable. Roll a d10 and consult the Dragon Spirits table to see what happens.
**Table: Dragon Spirits**
1–2 | Astalagan's Blessing. Aidron and the characters each regain 1d4 + 4 hit points as the bronze light surrounds them with warmth.
3–4 | Clyssavar's Flames. Sparkrender must succeed on a 12 Dexterity saving throw or take 7 (2d6) fire damage as the golden light crashes into him.
5–6 | Eldenemir's Gift. Sparkrender's Breath Weapon recharges as the blue light enfolds him.
7–8 | Sharruth's Fury. Each of the characters must succeed on a 12 Dexterity saving throw or take 3 (1d6) fire damage as the red light erupts with fire.
9–10 | Turadaer's Tricks. Aidron and the characters gain advantage on attack rolls and saving throws until initiative count 0 of the next round, as the brass light shimmers and sparks around them.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Freeing Aidron
children.content:
Aidron is bound by three heavy chains that keep him restrained. He can still take actions, such as biting or clawing an enemy that comes within his reach, but he knows Sparkrender is unaffected by his lightning Breath Weapon, so he doesn't bother using it. He might use his Repulsion Breath if he can see a good use for it.
Large clasps attach the chains to iron rings embedded in the ground. A character can use an action to undo one clasp. Once all three clasps are undone, Aidron is no longer restrained, though the chains reduce his speed by 10 feet. Removing the chains from Aidron takes 10 minutes.

[Freeing Aidron]: **[Overview]**: The text describes a specific scene within a larger location: a dragon named Aidron is chained within a space. The node title "Freeing Aidron" is an event/encounter, not a location itself. Its spatial context is the area where the chains are anchored.

**[Direct Connections]**: Not explicitly stated in this text. The area is presumably connected to the larger dungeon or lair containing it.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Aidron's Chains (Promoted)**: The immediate spatial feature described. Three heavy chains are attached via clasps to iron rings embedded in the ground of this chamber or cavern. This is the valid physical location of the encounter.
  - **Chain Anchor Points (A1, A2, A3)**: The three iron rings set into the floor, each with a heavy clasp securing a chain. A character can use an action to undo one clasp at each anchor point.

children.title: Disrupting the Ritual
children.content:
The most straightforward way to prevent Sparkrender from completing his ritual and obtaining the power he craves is to kill him. But characters can also use their actions in combat to interfere with the process and hinder Sparkrender in magical ways. Let the players try whatever they can imagine, using these ideas as examples:
- **Manipulate the Effigies**: A character might use an action to lift an effigy closer to the central sculpture, breathe a prayer to the dragon it represents, or otherwise coax magic from it. Doing so immediately triggers the corresponding effect from the Dragon Spirits Table.
- **Destroy the Effigies**: A character might break an effigy or throw it over the crumbling wall and into the ocean below. This ensures the corresponding effect does not occur again. (Reroll if you get that result on the Dragon Spirits table.)
- **Manipulate the Sculpture**: Characters might try to manipulate the golden sculpture as a way of disrupting the ritual. The sculpture is large and sturdy, though, so a single action has no noticeable effect on the sculpture or the magic. (The sculpture has AC 20, 27 hit points, and immunity to poison and psychic damage.) However, disturbing the sculpture does distract Sparkrender. The first time a character uses an action to attack or otherwise try to disturb the sculpture, the distracted blue dragon has disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws until the end of his next turn. Once he sees the character's meddling isn't very effective, he can't be distracted in this way again.

**[Direct Connections]**: No explicit exits, doors, or passages are mentioned in the text.

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **The Ruined Rotunda (D5)**: A circular, ruined chamber. Its central feature is a golden statue, around which streams of colored light (red, gold, brass, blue, bronze) swirl through the air. A blue dragon wyrmling (Sparkrender) is perched atop the statue, and a bronze dragon wyrmling (Aidron) is chained at its base.

children.title: Ending the Adventure
children.content:
With Aidron in tow, the characters can return victorious to Dragon's Rest. Runara is pleased by the bronze wyrmling's safe return. As a reward, she gives each of the characters a potion of healing and an exquisite pearl worth 100 gp. She welcomes them to stay at Dragon's Rest as long as they wish and furnishes them with whatever supplies they need for their travels when they're ready to leave the island. If Sparkrender is dead, she grieves the death of yet another dragon on Stormwreck Isle, but she doesn't condemn the characters for killing him.
If your players wish to continue playing their characters, you can use the contents of this set to create your own adventures. The "Exploring the Island" section of chapter 1 offers additional encounters you can use if the characters haven't already faced them. Perhaps Sparkrender (or a relative of his) pursues the characters in search of revenge, or perhaps something uncovered in the secret library leads the characters to a distant locale in pursuit of more adventure.
If you want to advance these characters beyond 3rd level and create adventures for them, you'll need the Basic Rules or the advanced rulebooks: the Player's Handbook, Dungeon Master's Guide, and Monster Manual.
children.spatial_summary:
[Ending the Adventure]: **[Overview]**: The final node describes the characters' return to the primary settlement of Stormwreck Isle, concluding the adventure. No new major locations are introduced here.

**[Direct Connections]**: The characters return to **Dragon's Rest** from elsewhere on the island (presumably from the **Seagrow Caves** or **Whispering Caverns**).

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
  - Dragon's Rest (Settlement): The primary hub on Stormwreck Isle. Runara's sanctuary where the characters are rewarded and can resupply.
    - The Cloister (Building): Runara's residence and the heart of Dragon's Rest.

spatial_summary:
[Chapter 4: Clifftop Observatory]: **[Overview]**: The spatial information from the children nodes describes the Clifftop Observatory ruins and their immediate surroundings on the southeastern coast of Stormwreck Isle. The current node, "Chapter 4: Clifftop Observatory," is a non-spatial, procedural chapter title. Therefore, no node is created for it, and all valid spatial children are promoted to the top level.

**[Direct Connections]**: (Not applicable to this non-spatial node)

**[Spatial Hierarchy]**:
- **Stormwreck Isle**: The larger island.
  - **Southeastern Coast**: The mainland shore where a boat can be beached. It connects to **D1. The Gilded Roar Overlook** via a climb up the bluffs.
  - **The Crystalline Scarlands**: A rocky, open area on the island's surface, marked by protrusions of glassy crystal and vegetation bearing reddish, crystalline scarring patterns. It connects to **D1. The Gilded Roar Overlook**.
  - **Clifftop Observatory Ruins**: A ruin complex built atop basalt columns offshore.
    - **The Basalt Column Ruins**: The collective term for the structures on the pillars. A boat can be tied at the base of a pillar to climb directly to the top of one.
      - **Pillar Top (D2, D3, D4, or D5)**: Accessible by climbing a specific column from a boat tied at its base.
    - **D1. The Gilded Roar Overlook**: A cliffside overlook marked by two gold-veined marble dragon statues. It is reached by a broken, overgrown path from the Scarlands or a climb from the Southeastern Coast. It connects to **D2. Rotunda Ruins** via a magical bridge.
      - **The Broken Approach (Path)**: The overgrown and broken path leading to the overlook.
    - **D2. Rotunda Ruins**: A large, ruined circular chamber or plaza, connected to D1 and D4 via magical bridges.
      - **The Shattered Plaza**: The main floor, paved with broken stone.
        - **The Golden Sculpture (Landmark)**: A large, rusted model of the solar system at the plaza's center.
        - **Dragon Effigy Circle**: A ritual site with five crudely painted stone dragon effigies arranged around the Golden Sculpture.
      - **Energy Bridge Anchors**: Four dragon statues around the perimeter; the western pair connects to D1, the southwestern pair connects to D4.
    - **D3. Kobold Camp**: A structure connected to the Rotunda (D2) by a rickety driftwood and rope bridge spanning a 15-foot gap.
      - **The Ruined Tower**: A dilapidated stone structure with gaps patched by wood and cloth.
    - **D4. The Isolated Study**: A study on a stone column, separated by a 22-foot gap from D2. Contains a hidden compartment in its northwest wall.
      - **Northwest Wall (Hidden Compartment)**: Contains a Potion of Lightning Resistance and a pouch of 10 gp.
    - **D5. Observatory Tower**: A circular chamber with crumbled walls and a stained glass dome, 45 feet above the ocean. Contains a gilded star map and a sleeping dragon.
      - **Statue Mechanism**: Four rotatable alabaster statues of scholars.
      - **Hidden Entrance / Stairwell Shaft**: A semi-circular section of floor that descends as a spiral staircase to D6.
      - **Collapsed Wall Section**: A caved-in portion of the wall providing rough access to D6.
    - **D6. Secret Library (Ruined Archive)**: A crumbling, hidden room filled with tomes and scrolls, accessible from D5 or from the water below. Contains a hidden +1 battleaxe and a *spell scroll of hold person*.
    - **A1. Chamber Below**: A lower chamber accessed via a vertical drop through the crumbled floor of a separate, ruined tower room overlooking the sea.
  - **The Ocean Surface**: The water 30-45 feet below the main ruins, accessible by falling or jumping. The cliffs allow for easy climbing back up.
- **Dragon's Rest (Settlement)**: The primary hub on Stormwreck Isle. Runara's sanctuary where the characters are rewarded and can resupply.
  - **The Cloister (Building)**: Runara's residence and the heart of Dragon's Rest.

title: Appendix A: Magic Items
content: Every adventure holds the promise—but not a guarantee—of finding one or more magic items. Dragons of Stormwreck Isle contains an assortment of magic items that hints at the wider variety of magic items waiting to be found in the worlds of D&D.

children.title: Using a Magic Item
children.content: A magic item's description explains how the item works. Handling a magic item is enough to give a character a sense that something is extraordinary about the item. To learn more, a character can concentrate on the item during a short rest, while being in physical contact with the item. At the end of the rest, the character learns the item's properties. Potions are an exception; a little taste is enough to tell the taster what the potion does.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

title: Appendix B: Creatures
content: This appendix contains stat blocks and short descriptions for the creatures that appear in Dragons of Stormwreck Isle.

children.title: Creature Stat Blocks
children.content: A creature's stat block provides the essential information that you, as the DM, need to run it. The following sections explain the various pieces of information you'll find in a stat block.

children.title: Size
children.content: A stat block tells you a creature's size: Tiny, Small, Medium, Large, Huge, or Gargantuan. Size is explained in the Basic Rules.

children.title: Squeezing into a Smaller Space
children.content: A creature can squeeze through a space large enough for a creature one size smaller than itself. When a creature is squeezing through such a space, its speed is halved, it has disadvantage on attack rolls and Dexterity saving throws, and attack rolls against it have advantage.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO
NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Type
children.content: A creature's type is specified in a stat block, indicating the family of creatures that it belongs to. The types in the game are Aberration, Beast, Celestial, Construct, Dragon, Elemental, Fey, Fiend, Giant, Humanoid, Monstrosity, Ooze, Plant, and Undead.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Alignment
children.content: A creature's alignment reflects its disposition. See Alignment in the Basic Rules for descriptions of the different alignments.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Armor Class
children.content:
A creature that wears armor or carries a shield has an AC that takes its armor, shield, and Dexterity into account. Otherwise, a creature's AC is based on its Dexterity modifier and any natural armor or supernatural resilience it might possess.
If a creature wears armor or carries a shield, the kind of armor it wears or shield it carries is noted in parentheses after its AC value.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Hit Points
children.content: A stat block specifies a creature's hit point maximum. In parentheses, the stat block also indicates the Hit Dice that were rolled to determined those hit points, plus the creature's Constitution modifier multiplied by the number of Hit Dice.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Speed
children.content:
A creature's speed tells you how far it can move on its turn. For more information on speed, see Speed in the Basic Rules.
All creatures have a walking speed; those that have no form of ground-based locomotion have a walking speed of 0 feet. Some of the creatures in this adventure have one or more additional movement modes:
- **Burrow**: A creature that has a burrowing speed can use all or part of its movement to move through sand, earth, mud, or ice. It can't burrow through solid rock unless it has a special trait that allows it to do so.
- **Fly**: A creature that has a flying speed can use all or part of its movement to fly. If the creature is incapacitated or knocked prone while flying, it falls unless it can hover.
- **Swim**: A creature that has a swimming speed doesn't need to spend extra movement to swim.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Ability Scores
children.content: Every creature has six ability scores (Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma) and corresponding modifiers. For more information on ability scores, see Ability Scores and Modifiers in the Basic Rules.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Saving Throws
children.content:
The Saving Throws entry is reserved for creatures that are proficient in one or more saving throws.
Saving throws in a stat block are shown with the total modifier—the creature's ability modifier plus its proficiency bonus. If a creature's stat block says "Con +4," roll a d20 and add 4 when the creature makes a saving throw using Constitution.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Skills
children.content:
The Skills entry is reserved for creatures that are proficient in one or more skills.
Skills in a stat block are shown with the total modifier—the creature's ability modifier plus its proficiency bonus. If a creature's stat block says "Stealth +4," roll a d20 and add 4 when the creature makes an ability check using Stealth.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Vulnerabilities, Resistances, and Immunities
children.content: Some creatures have vulnerability, resistance, or immunity to certain types of damage. Additionally, some creatures are immune to certain conditions. These immunities are also noted here.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Senses
children.content:
The Senses entry gives a creature's passive Wisdom (Perception) score, as well as any special senses the creature has, such as the following:
- **Blindsight**: A creature with blindsight can perceive its surroundings without having to rely on sight, within a specific radius.
- **Darkvision**: A creature with darkvision can see in dim light within the radius as if it were bright light and in darkness as if it were dim light. The creature discerns color in darkness as shades of gray.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Languages
children.content: The languages that a creature can speak are listed in alphabetical order.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Challenge
children.content: A well-rested party of four adventurers should be able to defeat a creature that has a challenge rating equal to the adventurers' level without any characters dying. Creatures weaker than 1st-level characters have challenge ratings lower than 1.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Traits
children.content: In a stat block, traits are special features that appear right under the creature's Challenge entry.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Actions
children.content: When a creature takes its action, it can choose from the options in the "Actions" section of its stat block and the options under Actions in the Basic Rules.

children.title: Melee and Ranged Attacks
children.content:
The most common actions that a creature takes in combat are melee and ranged attacks. See Making an Attack in the Basic Rules for how attacks work.
Any damage or other effects that occur as a result of an attack hitting a target are described here. As the DM, you can use the average damage or roll the damage; for this reason, both the average damage and the die expression are presented. For example, if a monster deals 4 (1d8) slashing damage with its longsword, that notation means you can have the monster deal 4 damage, or you can roll 1d8 to determine the damage.
children.spatial_summary: NO_SPATIAL_INFO
NO_SPATIAL_INFO

children.title: Limited Usage
children.content:
Some special abilities—whether they are traits, actions, or reactions—have restrictions on the number of times they can be used:
- **X/Day**: The notation "X/Day" means a special ability can be used a certain number of times, and then the creature must finish a long rest to regain expended uses. For example, "1/Day" means a special ability can be used once, and then the creature must finish a long rest to use it again.
Recharge X–Y. The notation "Recharge X–Y" means a creature can use a special ability once, and then the ability has a random chance of recharging during each subsequent round of combat. At the start of each of the creature's turns, roll a d6. If the roll is one of the numbers in the recharge notation, the creature regains the use of that special ability. The ability also recharges when the creature finishes a short or long rest. For example, "Recharge 5–6" means a creature can use the special ability once. Then, at the start of the creature's turn each round, it regains the use of the ability if it rolls a 5 or 6 on a d6.
